subUnits.title: 4.1 Price System and Demand: Meaning, Schedules, Curves and Laws
notes: The Price System:
• The mechanism by which prices are determined in a market economy
• Prices serve as signals that coordinate the actions of buyers and sellers
• Key functions of prices:
- Rationing: Allocating scarce resources among competing uses
- Signaling: Conveying information about scarcity and value
- Incentive: Encouraging production and consumption decisions
- Distribution: Determining who gets what goods and services
• Nigerian Example: Fuel prices signal scarcity and influence consumption and smuggling decisions
Demand:
• Definition: The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to buy at various prices during a given period
• Key Elements of Demand:
1. Willingness to buy: Desire for the good
2. Ability to pay: Sufficient income or purchasing power
3. Specific time period: Demand is always for a given time (day, month, year)
• Important: Demand is not the same as 'want'—it requires both desire and purchasing power
Demand Schedule:
• Definition: A table showing the relationship between price and quantity demanded
ASCII Diagram: Demand Schedule for Rice in Nigeria (per kg)
│  Price (₦)      │ Quantity Demanded (kg)│
• Interpretation: As price falls, quantity demanded increases (ceteris paribus)
Demand Curve:
• Definition: A graphical representation of the demand schedule
• Characteristics:
- Downward sloping from left to right
- Shows inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded
- Based on the law of demand
ASCII Diagram: Demand Curve
Price (₦)
• Downward slope reflects the law of demand
The Law of Demand:
• Statement: 'Other things being equal (ceteris paribus), as the price of a good falls, the quantity demanded rises, and as price rises, quantity demanded falls'
ASCII Diagram: Law of Demand Illustration
Price ↓ → Quantity Demanded ↑
Price ↑ → Quantity Demanded ↓
• Explanation: Why does the law of demand hold?
1. Substitution Effect:
- When price falls, the good becomes relatively cheaper than substitutes
- Consumers substitute toward the cheaper good
- Example: If rice price falls, consumers buy more rice instead of garri
2. Income Effect:
- When price falls, consumers' real income (purchasing power) increases
- They can afford to buy more of the good
- Example: Lower rice prices mean households can buy more rice with same income
Exceptions to the Law of Demand (Rare):
a) Giffen Goods:
- Inferior goods where income effect outweighs substitution effect
- Example: Very poor households buying more staple food when its price rises
b) Veblen Goods:
- Luxury goods where higher price increases prestige value
- Example: Designer clothes, luxury cars
c) Speculative Demand:
- When consumers expect future price changes
- Example: Buying more fuel now if expecting future price increase
Factors Affecting Demand (Determinants of Demand):
• These factors shift the entire demand curve (change in demand), as opposed to movement along the curve (change in quantity demanded)
1. Consumer Income:
• Normal Goods: Demand increases when income increases
- Example: Demand for rice, clothing, education increases with income
• Inferior Goods: Demand decreases when income increases
- Example: Demand for low-quality staples may fall as income rises
• Nigerian Context: As incomes rise, demand shifts from garri to rice to imported foods
2. Prices of Related Goods:
• Substitute Goods: Goods that can replace each other
- If price of substitute rises, demand for original good rises
- Example: If garri price rises, demand for rice rises
• Complementary Goods: Goods used together
- If price of complement rises, demand for original good falls
- Example: If fuel price rises, demand for cars may fall
3. Consumer Tastes and Preferences:
• Changes in fashion, advertising, culture affect demand
• Example: Increased health consciousness increases demand for organic foods
• Nigerian Example: Social media influence on demand for fashion items
4. Population and Demographics:
• Larger population → higher market demand
• Age structure, urbanization affect demand patterns
• Nigerian Example: Youthful population increases demand for education and technology
5. Expectations:
• Expectations about future prices, income, or availability
• Example: Expecting fuel price increase → buy more now (speculative demand)
6. Government Policy:
• Taxes, subsidies, regulations affect demand
• Example: Subsidy on fertilizer increases demand for agricultural inputs
ASCII Diagram: Movement Along vs Shift of Demand Curve
Movement Along Curve (Change in Quantity Demanded):
Price
│    D
P1│--*--  ← Price falls from P1 to P2
│   \   → Quantity demanded increases
Q1  Q2
Shift of Curve (Change in Demand):
Price
│    D1   D2
│  /    /   ← Income increases
│ /    /    → Demand increases at every price
• Visual Distinction:
- Movement along: Point moves along same curve
- Shift: Entire curve moves to new position
Nigerian Applications of Demand Analysis:
1. Agricultural Policy:
• Understanding demand for crops helps plan production and imports
• Example: Rising demand for rice informs policies on local production vs importation
2. Pricing Strategies:
• Businesses use demand analysis to set optimal prices
• Example: Telecom companies analyzing demand for data bundles at different prices
3. Tax Policy:
• Government considers demand elasticity when designing taxes
• Example: Taxing cigarettes (inelastic demand) vs luxury goods (elastic demand)
4. Subsidy Design:
• Subsidies target goods with high social value and elastic demand
• Example: Fertilizer subsidies to increase agricultural production
5. Consumer Protection:
• Understanding demand helps identify exploitative pricing
• Example: Monitoring prices of essential goods during scarcity
Common Misconceptions about Demand:
• 'Demand is the same as quantity demanded': Demand is the entire relationship; quantity demanded is a specific point
• 'Higher price always means lower demand': Price affects quantity demanded along a curve; other factors shift the curve
• 'Demand curve can slope upward': Only in rare exceptions; law of demand generally holds
• 'Demand is fixed': Demand changes with income, preferences, and other factors
Conclusion:
• The price system coordinates economic activity through supply and demand
• Demand represents consumers' willingness and ability to buy at various prices
• The law of demand describes the inverse price-quantity relationship
• Multiple factors influence demand, causing shifts in the demand curve
• Understanding demand is essential for business strategy, policy design, and personal decision-making in Nigeria's market economy
diagramDescriptions: Demand schedule table showing price-quantity relationship for rice
Demand curve graph: downward-sloping line with price and quantity axes
Diagram illustrating substitution and income effects of a price change
Graph showing movement along demand curve vs shift of demand curve
Nigerian example: Demand shifts for rice due to income growth or import policy changes
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Demand curve law of demand economics SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Demand in economics refers to:
opts: Willingness and ability to buy at various prices
Only the price of a good
quiz.explanation: Demand requires both the desire for a good and the purchasing power to buy it at different prices.

quiz.q: The law of demand states that, other things equal:
opts: Price and quantity demanded move in the same direction
Price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions
Price does not affect quantity demanded
quiz.explanation: The law of demand describes the inverse relationship: as price falls, quantity demanded rises, and vice versa.

quiz.q: A demand schedule is:
quiz.explanation: A demand schedule is a table that lists the quantity demanded at each possible price.

quiz.q: The demand curve slopes downward because:
opts: Producers supply less at higher prices
Consumers buy more at lower prices due to substitution and income effects
quiz.explanation: The downward slope reflects the law of demand: lower prices increase quantity demanded through substitution and income effects.

quiz.q: If the price of garri rises, the demand for rice (a substitute) will:
quiz.explanation: When the price of a substitute rises, consumers switch to the relatively cheaper alternative, increasing its demand.

quiz.q: An increase in consumer income will shift the demand curve for a normal good:
quiz.explanation: For normal goods, higher income increases demand at every price, shifting the entire demand curve to the right.

quiz.q: A movement along the demand curve is caused by:
opts: Change in the good's own price
quiz.explanation: Only a change in the good's own price causes movement along the demand curve; other factors shift the entire curve.

quiz.q: An example of a complementary good to fuel in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Cars and fuel are complements; if fuel prices rise, demand for cars may fall because operating costs increase.

quiz.q: The substitution effect of a price decrease means:
opts: The good becomes relatively cheaper than substitutes
quiz.explanation: When a good's price falls, it becomes relatively cheaper compared to substitutes, leading consumers to substitute toward it.

quiz.q: In Nigeria, understanding demand for rice is important for:
opts: Agricultural policy, pricing, and food security planning
quiz.explanation: Demand analysis informs multiple policy areas: production planning, import decisions, price controls, and food security strategies.

subUnits.title: 4.2 Factors Affecting Demand
notes: Factors Affecting Demand (Determinants of Demand):
• While price affects quantity demanded (movement along the curve), other factors shift the entire demand curve
• These determinants change demand at every price level
• Understanding them is crucial for predicting market changes and designing policies
1. Consumer Income:
• Relationship depends on type of good:
a) Normal Goods:
- Demand increases when income increases
- Examples: Rice, clothing, education, healthcare
- Nigerian Context: As incomes rise, demand shifts from local to imported goods
ASCII Diagram: Income Effect on Normal Good Demand
Price
│    D1   D2
│  /    /   ← Income increases
│ /    /    → Demand increases at every price
b) Inferior Goods:
- Demand decreases when income increases
- Examples: Low-quality staples, used clothing, public transport (for some)
- Nigerian Context: As incomes rise, some consumers shift from garri to rice, from bus to car
ASCII Diagram: Income Effect on Inferior Good Demand
Price
│    D2   D1
│  /    /   ← Income increases
│ /    /    → Demand decreases at every price
• Measuring Income Effect:
- Income elasticity of demand: % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in income
- Normal goods: Positive elasticity
- Inferior goods: Negative elasticity
2. Prices of Related Goods:
a) Substitute Goods:
- Definition: Goods that can replace each other in consumption
- Relationship: If price of substitute rises, demand for original good rises
ASCII Diagram: Substitute Goods Effect
Price of Rice
│    D1   D2
│  /    /   ← Price of garri rises
│ /    /    → Demand for rice increases
└──────────────→ Quantity of Rice
- Examples:
• Rice and garri
• Tea and coffee
• Bus and train transport
- Nigerian Example: If imported rice becomes expensive due to exchange rate, demand for local rice increases
b) Complementary Goods:
- Definition: Goods used together
- Relationship: If price of complement rises, demand for original good falls
ASCII Diagram: Complementary Goods Effect
Price of Cars
│    D2   D1
│  /    /   ← Price of fuel rises
│ /    /    → Demand for cars decreases
└──────────────→ Quantity of Cars
- Examples:
• Cars and fuel
• Phones and data plans
• Generators and fuel
- Nigerian Example: If fuel prices rise, demand for generators and cars may fall
3. Consumer Tastes and Preferences:
• Definition: Changes in what consumers like or desire
• Influenced by:
- Advertising and marketing
- Cultural trends and social media
- Health information and education
- Peer influence and fashion
• Examples:
- Increased health consciousness → higher demand for organic foods
- Social media trends → demand for specific fashion items
- Religious or cultural events → seasonal demand spikes
• Nigerian Context:
- Social media influencers affect demand for beauty products
- Health campaigns affect demand for tobacco or sugary drinks
- Cultural festivals increase demand for specific foods and clothing
4. Population and Demographics:
• Population Size:
- Larger population → larger market demand
- Example: Nigeria's growing population increases demand for food, housing, education
• Age Structure:
- Youthful population → higher demand for education, technology, entertainment
- Aging population → higher demand for healthcare, retirement services
- Nigerian Context: Nigeria's youthful population drives demand for education and digital services
• Urbanization:
- Urban dwellers have different demand patterns than rural
- Example: Higher demand for processed foods, transport, housing in cities
- Nigerian Context: Rural-urban migration increases demand for urban infrastructure
5. Expectations:
• Expectations about Future Prices:
- If consumers expect prices to rise, they buy more now (speculative demand)
- If they expect prices to fall, they delay purchases
- Nigerian Example: Expecting fuel price increase → buy more fuel now
• Expectations about Future Income:
- Expecting higher future income → increase current spending
- Expecting job loss → reduce current spending
- Nigerian Context: Economic uncertainty affects consumer confidence and spending
6. Government Policy:
• Taxes:
- Taxes on goods increase effective price, reducing demand
- Example: Excise duties on cigarettes reduce demand
• Subsidies:
- Subsidies lower effective price, increasing demand
- Example: Fertilizer subsidies increase demand for agricultural inputs
• Regulations:
- Bans, standards, or requirements affect demand
- Example: Ban on certain imports shifts demand to local alternatives
ASCII Diagram: Government Policy Effects on Demand
Price
│    D1   D2
│  /    /   ← Subsidy introduced
│ /    /    → Demand increases
Distinguishing Change in Demand vs Change in Quantity Demanded:
• Change in Quantity Demanded:
- Caused by change in the good's own price
- Movement along the same demand curve
- Example: Rice price falls → consumers buy more rice (movement down along demand curve)
• Change in Demand:
- Caused by change in any determinant other than own price
- Entire demand curve shifts right (increase) or left (decrease)
- Example: Income rises → demand for rice increases at every price (curve shifts right)
ASCII Diagram: Visual Identification Guide
Movement Along Curve:
Price
│    D
│  * ← Point moves on same curve
Shift of Curve:
Price
│    D1   D2
│  /    /   ← Entire curve moves
Applications in Nigerian Economic Analysis:
1. Agricultural Markets:
• Understanding demand determinants helps plan production and imports
• Example: Rising incomes and urbanization increase demand for processed foods
2. Import Policy:
• Exchange rate changes affect demand for imported goods
• Example: Naira depreciation reduces demand for imported vehicles
3. Tax and Subsidy Design:
• Policy makers consider demand elasticity when designing taxes/subsidies
• Example: Subsidizing essential goods with inelastic demand
4. Business Strategy:
• Firms monitor income trends, tastes, and expectations to forecast demand
• Example: Telecom companies adjust data bundle pricing based on income growth
5. Inflation Analysis:
• Demand-pull inflation occurs when aggregate demand exceeds supply
• Example: Excess demand for foreign exchange drives naira depreciation
Common Misconceptions:
• 'Higher price always reduces demand': Price affects quantity demanded along a curve; other factors shift the curve
• 'Demand is fixed': Demand changes with income, preferences, and other determinants
• 'All goods respond the same to income changes': Normal vs inferior goods respond differently
• 'Expectations don't matter': Expectations significantly influence current demand
Practical Exercise:
Activity: Analyze demand for a common good in your community (e.g., bread, fuel, mobile data)
Steps:
1. Identify the good and collect price-quantity data if possible
2. List factors that might affect its demand in your area
3. For each factor, predict how a change would shift demand
4. Draw demand curves showing predicted shifts
5. Discuss: Which factors are most important in your community? Why?
Conclusion:
• Demand is influenced by multiple factors beyond just price
• Understanding these determinants helps predict market changes and design effective policies
• In Nigeria's dynamic economy, income growth, urbanization, and policy changes constantly shift demand patterns
• Mastery of demand analysis is essential for business success, policy effectiveness, and informed citizenship
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing how income change shifts demand curve for normal vs inferior goods
Illustration of substitute and complement effects on demand curves
Graph showing movement along demand curve vs shift of demand curve
Nigerian example: Demand shifts for imported goods due to exchange rate changes
Flowchart: Factors affecting demand and their directional effects

quiz.q: An increase in consumer income will increase demand for:
quiz.explanation: Normal goods have positive income elasticity; demand increases when income increases.

quiz.q: If the price of a substitute good rises, the demand for the original good will:
quiz.explanation: Consumers substitute toward the relatively cheaper good, increasing its demand.

quiz.q: A change in consumer tastes favoring a good will shift its demand curve:
quiz.explanation: Increased preference increases demand at every price, shifting the entire curve to the right.

quiz.q: Population growth in Nigeria is likely to increase demand for:
opts: Most goods and services, especially basics
quiz.explanation: A larger population increases market size, raising demand for food, housing, education, and other goods.

quiz.q: If consumers expect the price of fuel to rise next week, current demand for fuel will likely:
quiz.explanation: Expectations of future price increases lead to speculative buying, increasing current demand.

quiz.q: A government subsidy on a good will shift its demand curve:
quiz.explanation: Subsidies lower the effective price to consumers, increasing demand at every market price.

quiz.q: A change in the good's own price causes:
opts: A shift of the demand curve
A movement along the demand curve
quiz.explanation: Only price changes cause movement along the demand curve; other factors shift the entire curve.

quiz.q: In Nigeria, urbanization increases demand for:
opts: Processed foods, transport, and urban housing
quiz.explanation: Urban dwellers have different consumption patterns, increasing demand for processed foods, transport, and urban services.

quiz.q: An inferior good is one for which demand:
quiz.explanation: Inferior goods have negative income elasticity; as incomes rise, consumers shift to higher-quality alternatives.

quiz.q: Understanding demand determinants helps policymakers to:
opts: Design effective taxes, subsidies, and development policies
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of demand drivers enables better policy design for agriculture, industry, trade, and social welfare.
